medications.indication_rationale: First-line preventive vasodilator for recurrent Raynaud's attacks, to be COMMENCED BY THE GP at the 3–5 day review — not initiated by the AI Doctor in this acute telehealth contact. The source note names 'calcium channel blockers (e.g. nifedipine)' for vasospasm prevention but specifies no dose; no dose is synthesised here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Caution with hypotension; the patient's BP was 140/85 (clinician-measured) so no immediate contraindication, but the GP should titrate and monitor

medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable for the ischaemic pain during the acute attack alongside rewarming; the AI Doctor may advise standard over-the-counter dosing per the packet. No specific dose is stated in the source, so none is prescribed here.

medications.drug_name: Systemic anticoagulation / thrombolysis for presumed embolism
medications.indication_rationale: There is no evidence of a macrovascular arterial occlusion (bilateral symmetrical reperfusing vasospasm, intact pulses on in-person exam). Empirically anticoagulating or thrombolysing for a presumed embolism would be a harmful error of commission — anticoagulation is only considered if the in-person assessment demonstrates a genuine thromboembolic occlusion.

medications.drug_name: Oral antibiotics for a digital ulcer infection
medications.indication_rationale: No ulcer, skin breakdown or signs of infection are present; prescribing antibiotics for a non-existent infected ulcer is an error of commission. Antibiotics apply only if an infected digital ulcer later develops (see safety-netting).

The ischaemic pain should settle with core rewarming and simple analgesia; reaching for opioids for a resolving vasospastic attack is disproportionate and inappropriate in an unsupervised telehealth contact.

Non-selective beta-blockers can provoke or worsen peripheral vasospasm and are best avoided in Raynaud's; flagged so the AI Doctor does not suggest one and so any existing beta-blocker would be reviewed by the GP.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rewarm the WHOLE body immediately, not just the hands — put on thick socks, a jumper and gloves, run warm (not hot) water over the wrists and hands, and have a hot decaffeinated drink to raise core temperature.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Raising core body temperature triggers reflex peripheral vasodilation and terminates the attack more effectively than warming the hands alone.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the home warmer and wear gloves before any cold exposure — going outside, and especially before reaching into the freezer.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cold avoidance is the primary non-pharmacological control for Raynaud's and reduces attack frequency and severity.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Continue to avoid tobacco and nicotine entirely.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Nicotine is a potent vasoconstrictor that worsens digital ischaemia; the patient is already a non-smoker, which should be reinforced.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once the GP starts a preventive calcium-channel blocker, take it regularly through the colder months.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent preventive therapy reduces the frequency and severity of secondary Raynaud's attacks.

patient_education_points.point: Warm your whole body — extra layers plus a hot drink — not just your hands; raising your core temperature is what opens the blood vessels back up.

This is a severe blood-vessel spasm, not necessarily a permanent clot or blockage — but because I can't examine your pulses over a screen, you need to be seen in person tonight to be sure the circulation is recovering.
Watch your fingers closely: if any fingertip stays completely white or blue and numb for more than two hours despite warming, or a finger turns black, go to the emergency department.

See your GP within a few days to start a medication that prevents these spasms and to check for an underlying cause, because the skin tightening and reflux suggest this may be more than simple Raynaud's.
Avoid the cold and wear gloves before going outside or reaching into the freezer; avoid caffeine, and let your doctor know before starting any medication that could tighten blood vessels.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent in-person assessment tonight (after-hours GP / urgent care) while rewarming; then GP review within 3–5 days to commence preventive therapy and arrange work-up for the underlying cause.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Recovery of digital perfusion and colour, exclusion of critical/fixed ischaemia and arterial occlusion, skin and digit examination, and screening for underlying systemic sclerosis (antibodies, capillaroscopy).
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any fingertip remaining completely white/blue and numb for more than two hours despite core rewarming, a finger turning black, worsening or spreading pain, or an open sore developing on a fingertip.

must_include_items: Instruct immediate WHOLE-BODY rewarming (layers, warm not hot water, hot drink), not just warming the hands
Recognise this as a severe, prolonged, ischaemic digital attack that is beyond the patient's usual pattern
Arrange URGENT in-person assessment tonight because telehealth cannot palpate pulses or exclude critical digital ischaemia / arterial occlusion
Give an explicit, time-bound safety-net: emergency department if any fingertip stays white/blue and numb >2 hours despite warming, or turns black
Advise GP follow-up within a few days for preventive medication and work-up of the underlying cause (secondary-Raynaud's clues present)
acceptable_alternatives: Directing the patient to the emergency department rather than after-hours GP/urgent care as the in-person venue is acceptable given the diagnostic uncertainty
Recommending simple over-the-counter analgesia for pain alongside rewarming
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any in-person assessment (treating it as pure self-care)
Failing to provide a time-bound critical-ischaemia safety-net
Failing to flag the need for work-up of an underlying cause
errors_of_commission: Calling 000 / dispatching an ambulance for a resolving bilateral vasospasm with returning perfusion (over-triage)
Starting anticoagulation or thrombolysis for a presumed embolism
Prescribing antibiotics for a non-existent infected ulcer
Claiming to have EXCLUDED arterial occlusion or critical ischaemia remotely
Guaranteeing the pain will fully resolve within 15 minutes of rewarming
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both the whole-body rewarming instruction AND arrangement of urgent in-person assessment with a time-bound critical-ischaemia safety-net. Either over-triaging to an ambulance/000 or under-triaging to self-care only is a triage failure regardless of other content.
